title: Licania brittoniana
taxonomy: Licania brittoniana is classified within the family Sapindaceae, which is a family of flowering plants commonly known as the soapberry or lychee family. This family consists of about 140 genera and approximately 1,200 species. Licania brittoniana itself is a species within the genus Licania, which includes around 100 species of trees and shrubs native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The genus Licania is part of the order Sapindales, which comprises plants with small, often fragrant flowers. The classification of Licania brittoniana reflects its relationship to other plants in the Sapindaceae family and its unique characteristics.
uses: Licania brittoniana has been traditionally used in folk medicine in the tropical regions of Central and South America. Its bark and leaves are believed to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may aid in treating various health issues. In some cultures, the plant's extracts are used to treat skin conditions such as eczema and dermatitis. The plant's antimicrobial properties are also thought to help in wound healing and preventing infections. However, further research is needed to confirm the medicinal efficacy and safety of Licania brittoniana.

Some studies suggest that extracts from Licania brittoniana may have anti-inflammatory properties. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions.
